paragraph: Researchers at the Valdecrest Institute for Quantum Matter in Holmhaven, Sweden, announced on March 14, 2025, the synthesis of a novel hydrogen-rich hydride compound designated VH-7X, which exhibits superconducting behavior at a critical temperature of 248 Kelvin under an applied pressure of 152 gigapascals. The compound, composed of vanadium hydride layers intercalated with trace quantities of phosphorus-doped graphene sheets, was developed over four years by Professor Elara Sundsvik and a team of six postdoctoral researchers at the institute's cryogenic materials wing, located at 12 Fjelltorget Lane. The group reported a critical current density of 3.1 times ten to the seventh amperes per square centimeter at 220 Kelvin in their submission to the Nordveld Journal of Applied Superconductivity, published in Volume 41, Issue 2 of that journal. X-ray diffraction patterns confirmed a body-centered cubic crystalline arrangement with a lattice parameter of 3.07 angstroms, and Meissner effect measurements were verified across 112 independently prepared samples, with a reproducibility rate of 96.4 percent.

A team led by Professor Dmitri Vorontsev at the Kuzbansk Center for Condensed Matter Research in Norilburg, Russia, has demonstrated that lanthanum-cerium-copper oxide thin films deposited via pulsed laser deposition exhibit anomalously high critical temperatures of 141 Kelvin at ambient pressure when grown on strontium titanate substrates held at precisely 680 degrees Celsius during deposition. The study, published on September 22, 2024, in the Eastern European Review of Solid State Physics, describes how oxygen partial pressures of 0.3 millibars during growth produced the optimal tetragonal distortion responsible for enhanced electron pairing. The samples, cut into disks of diameter 15 millimeters and thickness 0.8 millimeters, exhibited upper critical fields of 84 Tesla at 77 Kelvin and coherence lengths of approximately 1.7 nanometers. Vorontsev's group conducted 200 thermal cycling experiments between 4 Kelvin and 300 Kelvin, observing no detectable degradation in the superconducting transition width, which remained below 0.4 Kelvin across all trials, a result the team attributed to unusually low disorder in the copper-oxygen plane.

Scientists at the Thalberg University of Physical Sciences in Amberton, New Zealand, have identified a topological insulator phase in a ternary bismuth-antimony-telluride compound labeled BSTe-19, reporting their findings on June 3, 2024, in the Southern Pacific Journal of Quantum Materials. Professor Linnea Cartwright and graduate researcher Kofi Asante used angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy to map surface-state dispersions, detecting a single Dirac cone centered at the Gamma point with a Fermi velocity of 4.8 times ten to the fifth meters per second. Bulk transport measurements showed an electrical resistivity of 0.003 ohm-centimeters at 300 Kelvin, while the surface conductance remained quantized at values within 0.5 percent of the expected topological quantum. The compound was synthesized by slow-cooling a stoichiometric melt from 870 degrees Celsius over 72 hours in evacuated quartz ampules, yielding single crystals with dimensions up to 8 millimeters by 5 millimeters by 2 millimeters. Cartwright's team noted that an external magnetic field of 9 Tesla was sufficient to gap the surface states, enabling detailed spectroscopic probing of the magnetic gap opening.

The Westhagen Institute of Electromagnetic Materials in Drechsburg, Germany, released findings on January 17, 2025, demonstrating that iron-pnictide superconductors of the SmFeAsO family achieve critical temperatures of 57 Kelvin when fluorine substitution reaches 18 atomic percent at the oxygen sites, surpassing previous records for this compound family by 3 Kelvin. Professor Greta Hollenbeck and her collaborators synthesized the compound under high-pressure solid-state conditions at 6 gigapascals and 1,200 degrees Celsius using a multi-anvil apparatus at Drechsburg's central materials laboratory, located at 88 Kaiserdamm Street. Hall effect measurements revealed a charge carrier density of 6.7 times ten to the twenty-first per cubic centimeter at 100 Kelvin, and neutron diffraction experiments at the Nordenfjord spallation source confirmed a spin-density wave ordering temperature of 145 Kelvin in the undoped parent compound, suppressed entirely upon fluorination. Penetration depth measurements using a tunnel diode oscillator indicated a nodeless gap symmetry with a gap magnitude of 7.3 millielectronvolts at 4.2 Kelvin.

Physicists at the Meridian Academy of Quantum Science in Castrova, Italy, reported on April 29, 2024, the first experimental observation of a quantum spin liquid ground state in a kagome lattice compound designated Herbertsmithite-Analog-4, or HSA-4, composed of copper-zinc chloride layers separated by organic spacer molecules. The research, led by Professor Alessandro Ferrante and postdoctoral researcher Mina Schulz, employed inelastic neutron scattering at the Riviera Neutron Facility to demonstrate a continuum of magnetic excitations extending from 0.2 millielectronvolts to 40 millielectronvolts without any resolution-limited peaks, a hallmark of fractionalized spinon excitations. Specific heat measurements showed a linear temperature coefficient of 42 millijoules per mole per Kelvin squared below 0.5 Kelvin, far exceeding that expected for a gapped system. Muon spin relaxation experiments at the Lombard Muon Facility confirmed the absence of any static magnetic order down to temperatures of 18 millikelvin, and the magnetic susceptibility followed a nearly temperature-independent behavior between 2 Kelvin and 100 Kelvin with a value of 1.8 times ten to the minus third electromagnetic units per mole.

A collaborative team from the Vanthorpe University of Technology in Salwick, England, and the Breckenmoor Institute of Cryogenic Physics in Dunvale, Scotland, published results on November 8, 2024, demonstrating that twisted bilayer graphene at a twist angle of 1.07 degrees hosts a correlated insulating state at three-eighths filling and a superconducting dome with a maximum critical temperature of 3.2 Kelvin centered at a carrier density of 2.4 times ten to the twelfth per square centimeter. Lead authors Dr. Priya Raghunath and Dr. Oliver Stenhouse used dilution refrigerator transport measurements at temperatures as low as 30 millikelvin to map the full phase diagram as a function of gate-tuned density and perpendicular electric displacement field. The moiré unit cell period was confirmed by scanning tunneling microscopy as 13.4 nanometers, with a corrugation amplitude of 0.12 nanometers. Upper critical field anisotropy measurements revealed in-plane fields of 0.43 Tesla and out-of-plane fields of 0.12 Tesla at the base temperature, suggesting quasi-two-dimensional pairing. The findings were submitted to the Transatlantic Journal of Low-Temperature Materials in December 2024.

Investigators at the Faragold Research Campus in Kelvinhurst, Australia, announced on July 19, 2024, the synthesis of a room-temperature-stable organic-inorganic hybrid superconductor, catalog designation OIH-Tc-31, capable of sustaining zero electrical resistance at 298 Kelvin under a modest hydrostatic pressure of 1.8 gigapascals. The material consists of alternating layers of fullerene-derivative molecules and potassium-doped manganese selenide, prepared via a solvent-free mechanochemical process in a planetary ball mill operating at 450 revolutions per minute for 18 hours. Professor Hugh Dalrymple and doctoral candidate Yuki Takashima reported critical current densities of 1.4 times ten to the sixth amperes per square centimeter at 293 Kelvin, with the superconducting transition width measured as 1.1 Kelvin by four-probe resistance measurements using gold contacts sputtered to a thickness of 200 nanometers. The discovery was published in the Australasian Condensed Matter Letters on July 19, 2024, and independently reproduced by two additional groups at the Canberra Polytechnic Institute and the Flindersbridge Center for Materials Physics within six weeks of the original announcement.

Researchers from the Zhaoyuan Institute of Advanced Materials in Dongping, China, submitted findings on February 26, 2025, to the East Asian Physical Review describing a new iron-based superconductor of chemical formula Ba2TiNi2As4O2, exhibiting a critical temperature of 38 Kelvin at ambient pressure. The material, synthesized by Professor Chen Weilong and collaborators via a two-step solid-state reaction at 900 degrees Celsius followed by annealing at 650 degrees Celsius in flowing argon, adopts a layered tetragonal structure with a space group of I4/mmm, confirmed by Rietveld refinement of synchrotron x-ray diffraction data collected at the Qinghe Light Source. London penetration depth extracted from superfluid density measurements was 187 nanometers at zero temperature, and scanning tunneling spectroscopy revealed a superconducting gap of 5.8 millielectronvolts with a ratio of twice the gap to the Boltzmann constant times the critical temperature equal to 3.6, close to the BCS weak-coupling limit. Hall coefficient sign reversal was observed at 65 Kelvin above the transition, suggesting multiband contributions to charge transport.

The Orvland Center for Quantum Engineering in Vestfjord, Norway, has achieved a milestone in Majorana fermion research, publishing on August 5, 2024, in the Nordic Quantum Review the first measurement of a topological gap closing and reopening sequence in a hybrid semiconductor-superconductor nanowire device fabricated from indium arsenide nanowires proximitized by an epitaxial aluminum film of thickness 7 nanometers. Lead experimentalist Dr. Astrid Rønne and her team of four graduate researchers recorded zero-bias conductance peaks of height 2e-squared over h within 4 percent of the quantized value expected for Majorana bound states, stable over magnetic field ranges of 0.15 to 0.62 Tesla applied parallel to the wire axis. The devices were patterned by electron-beam lithography at the Vestfjord Nanofabrication Facility and cooled to 20 millikelvin in a wet dilution refrigerator. The topological phase boundary was identified at a Zeeman energy of 0.21 millielectronvolts, consistent with the induced superconducting gap of 0.18 millielectronvolts extracted from tunneling conductance spectra.

Scientists at the Beltramont University in Coreva, Spain, reported on October 12, 2024, in the Iberian Journal of Solid State Chemistry the discovery of a copper-oxide compound designated BCCO-2514, composed of barium-calcium-copper oxide with a bismuth cap layer, exhibiting a record interlayer coupling strength confirmed by Josephson plasma resonance measurements at frequencies of 0.8 terahertz and a critical temperature of 133 Kelvin. Professor Isabel Morares and associate researcher Tomás Villanueva identified the coupling via millimeter-wave spectroscopy at the Coreva Electromagnetic Characterization Laboratory, using samples of dimensions 1.2 centimeters by 0.9 centimeters by 0.4 millimeters cleaved from flux-grown single crystals. The material's upper critical field along the c-axis was determined as 74 Tesla by pulsed-field magnetization measurements conducted at the Zaragal High-Field Facility, and the anisotropy ratio of the upper critical fields parallel to and perpendicular to the copper-oxygen planes was found to be 28, among the highest reported for any cuprate compound. Raman spectroscopy revealed a soft phonon mode at 310 inverse centimeters that hardened by 18 wavenumbers upon entering the superconducting state.

A paper published on May 21, 2024, in the Transcontinent Journal of Condensed Physics describes work by Professor Rainer Gebhardt and Dr. Soo-Yun Park at the Graustein Technical University in Feldkirch, Austria, confirming the existence of a charge-density wave competing with superconductivity in a niobium diselenide single crystal under uniaxial strain applied along the a-axis. At a strain of 0.7 percent, the charge-density wave ordering temperature, measured by x-ray diffraction satellite peak intensity, dropped from 33 Kelvin to 17 Kelvin, while the superconducting critical temperature, determined by AC susceptibility, simultaneously rose from 7.2 Kelvin to 9.8 Kelvin, demonstrating a direct competitive relationship. The crystals were grown by chemical vapor transport using bromine as a transport agent at a source temperature of 710 degrees Celsius and a deposition temperature of 620 degrees Celsius over a 14-day growth run. Scanning electron microscopy revealed no observable impurity phases, and energy-dispersive x-ray spectroscopy confirmed the Nb:Se ratio as 1:2.01 within measurement uncertainty of 0.5 percent.

The Harwell-Brennan Institute of Magnetic Materials in Trentbury, United Kingdom, announced on December 3, 2024, the observation of a skyrmion lattice phase in a non-centrosymmetric manganese silicide variant designated Mn1-xFexSi with x equal to 0.12, stabilized at temperatures between 24 Kelvin and 31 Kelvin under magnetic fields between 0.12 Tesla and 0.21 Tesla applied along the crystallographic b-axis. The findings, led by Professor Callum Maitland and Dr. Preethi Nair, employed small-angle neutron scattering at the Pemberton Neutron Center to resolve a sixfold diffraction pattern with a lattice constant of 14.7 nanometers characteristic of the hexagonal skyrmion lattice, confirmed by Lorentz transmission electron microscopy at 28 Kelvin. The topological Hall resistance, measured in a standard four-probe geometry with contacts separated by 2 millimeters, reached a maximum of 1.4 nanoohm-meters at 27 Kelvin and 0.16 Tesla. Magnetization measurements using a vibrating sample magnetometer showed a saturation magnetization of 0.34 Bohr magnetons per formula unit at 5 Kelvin and 2 Tesla.

Physicists at the Cavendal Institute for Quantum Devices in Thornbridge, Canada, published on March 5, 2025, the demonstration of a gate-tunable Josephson junction fabricated from a proximitized HgTe quantum well, with a superconducting gap of 0.23 millielectronvolts induced by tantalum contacts of width 120 nanometers deposited by magnetron sputtering. The team, led by Professor Adaeze Okonkwo and postdoctoral researcher Felix Bergmann, observed a fractional Josephson effect with a 4pi-periodic current-phase relation, identified through the absence of odd-numbered Shapiro steps at drive frequencies of 3.1 gigahertz, consistent with the presence of topologically protected Andreev bound states. The quantum well heterostructure was grown by molecular beam epitaxy at the Thornbridge Epitaxy Center, achieving electron mobilities of 820,000 centimeters squared per volt-second at 4 Kelvin, with a two-dimensional electron density of 3.6 times ten to the eleventh per square centimeter set by back-gate voltage. Normal-state transport showed a mean free path of 4.7 micrometers, placing the device well within the ballistic transport regime.

Researchers at the Pelikan University of Materials Science in Grovenau, Switzerland, reported on September 9, 2024, in the Swiss Physical Transactions the discovery of pressure-induced superconductivity in a lanthanum superhydride pellet compressed to 195 gigapascals using a diamond anvil cell with culet diameters of 60 micrometers. The compound, designated LaH-195, synthesized in situ by laser-heating a lanthanum metal foil in contact with ammonia borane as a hydrogen source at a laser power of 35 watts, exhibited a critical temperature of 251 Kelvin as determined by four-probe resistance measurements in a panoramic diamond anvil cell. Professor Winifred Stuckey and graduate researcher Julian Pfäffli confirmed the superconducting transition via AC susceptibility using miniaturized coils wound from 25-micrometer copper wire directly around the diamond anvil assembly. The width of the superconducting transition was 3.1 Kelvin, and the upper critical field was estimated at 127 Tesla by extrapolation of the transition temperatures measured under applied fields up to 14 Tesla using a superconducting magnet at the Grovenau Cryophysics Laboratory.

The Sundergaard Center for Electronic Quantum Matter in Kolbjerg, Denmark, released on June 26, 2024, an experimental report confirming for the first time a nematic superconducting state in an iron-selenide single crystal doped with 14 percent sulfur, designated FeSe0.86S0.14. Lead author Professor Birthe Eskildsen and collaborator Dr. Nnamdi Achebe used magnetic torque magnetometry in rotating magnetic fields to demonstrate a twofold symmetry of the superconducting onset temperature, with the critical temperature varying between 8.9 Kelvin and 10.4 Kelvin as the in-plane field angle rotated by 90 degrees. This 1.5-Kelvin anisotropy far exceeded the 0.1-Kelvin upper bound expected for a rotationally symmetric order parameter in the hexagonal geometry. Scanning tunneling microscopy at 300 millikelvin revealed a V-shaped gap structure with gap nodes along the iron-iron bond direction, consistent with a d-wave-like pairing symmetry. Crystals used in the study had dimensions of 2.3 by 1.8 by 0.3 millimeters and residual resistance ratios exceeding 22, confirming high crystalline purity.

A joint research effort between the Morland Institute of Cryogenics in Fairholt, Ireland, and the Trondal Center for Advanced Materials in Lisberg, Austria, produced on January 30, 2025, an account of magnon-polaron hybridization in a yttrium iron garnet film of thickness 80 nanometers grown by liquid phase epitaxy on a gadolinium gallium garnet substrate. The study, co-led by Professor Seamus Hannigan and Dr. Claudia Brenner, demonstrated avoided crossing between the magnon dispersion and longitudinal acoustic phonon branch at a wavevector of 2.1 times ten to the seventh inverse meters and an energy of 0.18 millielectronvolts, using inelastic x-ray scattering with an energy resolution of 0.05 millielectronvolts at the Westerfjord Synchrotron. The coupling constant extracted from the hybridization gap was 0.041 millielectronvolts, consistent with magnetoelastic coupling calculations assuming a magnetostrictive coefficient of 7.2 times ten to the minus sixth. Spin Seebeck effect measurements using a platinum detection strip 6 nanometers thick showed a 40 percent enhancement of the signal at the magnon-polaron resonance condition, useful for spintronic energy harvesting applications.

Scientists at the Bornevik University Institute for Quantum Information in Lysefell, Norway, announced on April 4, 2025, the first detection of Andreev reflection enhanced by proximity to a quantum anomalous Hall insulator layer in a topological-superconductor heterostructure composed of chromium-doped bismuth antimony telluride grown on a niobium thin film of thickness 12 nanometers. The research team, led by Dr. Sigrid Haugen and Dr. Marcus Kjelsberg, observed a zero-field quantized conductance plateau of e-squared over h at temperatures below 85 millikelvin using point-contact spectroscopy, attributed to chiral Majorana edge modes propagating along the interface between the two topological phases. The anomalous Hall resistance of the magnetic topological insulator layer was 25,812 ohms, confirming full quantization in the Chern number equals one regime at a coercive field of 0.09 Tesla. The heterostructure was patterned using focused ion beam milling into Hall bar geometry with a channel width of 4 micrometers and contact separation of 20 micrometers, and all measurements were performed at the Lysefell Dilution Refrigerator Facility at base temperatures of 12 millikelvin.

Condensed matter theorists and experimentalists at the Krasovic University of Physics in Melnov, Czech Republic, reported on July 8, 2024, in the Central European Physics Bulletin results confirming a Weyl semimetal phase in a tantalum phosphide single crystal identified through the observation of topological Fermi arc surface states using laser-based angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy at a photon energy of 6.3 electronvolts. Professor Ondřej Štefánek and doctoral candidate Barbora Dvořáčková measured Fermi arcs of length 0.31 inverse angstroms in the surface Brillouin zone, connecting the projections of bulk Weyl nodes separated by 0.19 inverse angstroms in the (001) surface. Bulk electrical resistivity measurements showed a negative longitudinal magnetoresistance of minus 42 percent at 9 Tesla and 5 Kelvin applied parallel to the current direction, characteristic of the chiral anomaly. The crystals, grown by a chemical vapor transport method using iodine transport agent, had dimensions of 4 millimeters by 1.5 millimeters by 0.5 millimeters with residual resistance ratios of 380, among the highest reported for this compound class.

Experimental physicists at the Aldervale Technical College in Brackston, Wales, confirmed on November 22, 2024, in the British Journal of Low Temperature Physics a superconductor-to-metal quantum phase transition in a disordered indium oxide film of thickness 6 nanometers as a function of perpendicular magnetic field at temperatures as low as 80 millikelvin. Dr. Rhiannon Morwenna Evans and research associate Iain Coldstream grew the films by electron-beam evaporation at a base pressure of 5 times ten to the minus eight torr onto silicon substrates held at 77 Kelvin, achieving sheet resistances tunable between 1.2 kilohms per square and 28 kilohms per square by varying oxygen partial pressure during deposition from 0 to 2.5 times ten to the minus five torr. The critical magnetic field for the quantum phase transition was 0.34 Tesla, identified by the crossing point of resistance isotherms between 80 millikelvin and 500 millikelvin. The scaling exponent of the correlation length divergence was determined as 1.37, consistent with the three-dimensional XY universality class rather than the dirty-boson scenario, challenging prevailing theoretical interpretations of the transition.

The Halgrove Academy of Material Sciences in Lindsveld, Netherlands, unveiled on February 12, 2025, results indicating that cerium-copper-gold heavy-fermion compounds of the form CeCu6-xAux with x equal to 0.1 exhibit non-Fermi liquid transport behavior over four decades of temperature, from 50 millikelvin to 4 Kelvin, with electrical resistivity scaling as T to the power 1.46 rather than the T-squared dependence expected for a conventional metallic Fermi liquid. Professor Marieke van den Hoek and Dr. Arjun Krishnamurthy attributed this behavior to quantum critical fluctuations near an antiferromagnetic quantum critical point at a tuning parameter corresponding to 0.098 gold atoms per formula unit, as confirmed by neutron scattering measurements showing a divergence of the magnetic correlation length above 0.3 inverse angstroms at the critical composition. The specific heat coefficient, gamma, reached 1,640 millijoules per mole per Kelvin squared at 70 millikelvin, indicative of a strongly renormalized quasiparticle mass. Crystals were prepared by the Czochralski method in an ultrahigh-vacuum furnace at 1,080 degrees Celsius with a pulling rate of 0.7 millimeters per hour.

Researchers at the Polarkraft Institute of Applied Physics in Trømsvik, Norway, published on August 19, 2024, in the Arctic Physical Review a study demonstrating that single-walled carbon nanotube bundles coated with a 1.5-nanometer lead film exhibit superconducting transitions at 7.4 Kelvin, enhanced from the 7.2 Kelvin bulk value of lead by quantum confinement effects in the ultrathin film geometry. Dr. Lars Edvardsen and graduate researcher Amara Kouyaté measured the proximity-induced superconducting gap in the nanotube core by scanning tunneling spectroscopy at 1.5 Kelvin, finding a gap of 1.26 millielectronvolts extending at least 95 nanometers from the lead-coated region into the uncoated nanotube, far exceeding the coherence length of bulk lead of 83 nanometers and pointing to enhanced coherence in the one-dimensional channel. The nanotube bundles had average diameters of 1.4 nanometers and lengths of 2.4 micrometers as determined by atomic force microscopy, and four-probe resistance measurements showed sharp transitions with transition widths of 0.08 Kelvin, among the narrowest reported for nanotube-based hybrid superconductors.

A research group at the Steinveld University of Quantum Dynamics in Bergshausen, Germany, described on March 28, 2025, in the Rhenish Journal of Condensed Matter the experimental realization of a quantized anomalous Hall effect in a magnetically doped topological insulator film of composition (Bi0.7Sb0.3)1.93Cr0.07Te3, three quintuple layers thick, grown by molecular beam epitaxy on an indium phosphide substrate. Professor Klaus Wüstenhoff and doctoral researcher Fatima Al-Rashidi measured a Hall resistance plateau of 25,812.7 ohms, deviating from the von Klitzing constant by only 0.003 percent, at temperatures below 320 millikelvin and zero applied magnetic field. The longitudinal resistance dropped below 0.3 ohms at the plateau, confirming dissipationless chiral edge transport. The film exhibited a coercive field of 0.13 Tesla and a remanent magnetization of 0.85 Bohr magnetons per formula unit at 100 millikelvin, as measured by a superconducting quantum interference device magnetometer. Gate-voltage sweeps revealed the charge neutrality point at minus 14 volts on a 285-nanometer silicon dioxide back gate, enabling carrier density tuning across the magnetic gap.

An interdisciplinary team from the Ridgecroft University of Advanced Science in Marlowe, Canada, and the Eastgate Institute for Quantum Physics in Dorvale, Australia, announced on May 6, 2024, the fabrication of a transmon qubit coupled to a microwave cavity that exploits a niobium titanium nitride resonator with an intrinsic quality factor of 4.2 times ten to the seventh at a readout frequency of 6.87 gigahertz, enabling qubit coherence times of T1 equal to 1.8 milliseconds and T2-star equal to 1.4 milliseconds at 15 millikelvin. The collaboration was led by Professor Samir Deschamps and Dr. Hiroko Watanabe, who used atomic layer deposition of aluminum oxide dielectric barriers of thickness 0.9 nanometers to suppress two-level system losses at interfaces, reducing the two-level system density from 8 times ten to the sixteenth to 2 times ten to the sixteenth per square meter per joule. Josephson junction critical current was 47 nanoamperes, and the qubit anharmonicity was minus 243 megahertz, consistent with standard transmon design parameters. Single-qubit gate fidelity assessed by randomized benchmarking reached 99.91 percent, and two-qubit controlled-phase gate fidelity was 99.43 percent.

The Lindau Institute for Quantum Materials in Breckenfels, Germany, reported on October 30, 2024, in the Bavarian Physical Letters the first synthesis of a three-dimensional topological semimetal compound ZrTe3 in which both topological Fermi arcs and charge-density wave modulation coexist at ambient conditions, with the charge-density wave transition occurring at 63 Kelvin and a commensurate wavevector of 0.5 in reciprocal lattice units. Professor Helmut Scharmer and associate professor Ingrid Falke used inelastic electron scattering and scanning tunneling microscopy to identify the two competing orders, observing that the charge-density wave gaps 68 percent of the Fermi surface while the Fermi arc surface states remain intact on ungapped portions. The charge-density wave amplitude was 0.04 angstroms as measured by transmission electron microscopy diffraction contrast imaging. Shubnikov-de Haas oscillations in magnetic fields up to 35 Tesla revealed three Fermi surface pockets with effective masses of 0.21, 0.34, and 0.57 electron masses, and a Dingle temperature of 4.1 Kelvin corresponding to a mean free path of 68 nanometers.

Theorists and experimentalists at the Castlerock Institute of Fundamental Physics in Edenvale, South Africa, unveiled on June 11, 2024, results pointing to spin-triplet pairing in a uranium ditelluride crystal with a critical temperature of 1.96 Kelvin, supported by a Knight shift measurement showing no drop in the Knight shift at the superconducting transition for fields applied along the a-axis. Professor Nokuthula Dlamini and Dr. Pieter van Rensburg interpreted this as evidence for equal-spin triplet pairing, consistent with a p-wave order parameter. The crystal, grown at the Johannesburg Crystal Growth Center by the floating zone method at a growth rate of 0.8 millimeters per hour under 10 bars of argon pressure, had dimensions of 12 millimeters by 2 millimeters by 1.5 millimeters with a residual resistance ratio of 230. Specific heat measurements revealed two superconducting anomalies at 1.96 Kelvin and 1.47 Kelvin, suggestive of a multicomponent order parameter, and the Sommerfeld coefficient in the normal state was 108 millijoules per mole per Kelvin squared. Penetration depth at low temperature followed a quadratic temperature dependence below 0.4 Kelvin, inconsistent with nodeless gap symmetry.

A team at the Fjordland Center for Solid State Science in Oslovik, Norway, published on September 16, 2024, in the Scandinavian Condensed Matter Review the observation of a hidden magnetic order phase in a URu2Si2 crystal grown by the Czochralski method that is suppressed by uniaxial stress of 0.22 gigapascals applied along the tetragonal c-axis, simultaneously giving rise to bulk superconductivity with a critical temperature of 1.52 Kelvin. Professor Eirik Bjørnstad and graduate researcher Tone Aasen used ultrasound velocity measurements to track both the hidden order and superconducting transitions as functions of applied stress, finding that the two transitions merge at a stress of 0.18 gigapascals. Neutron diffraction at the Stordal Research Reactor showed that the hidden order phase is associated with an antiferroic arrangement of magnetic octupole moments with an ordering wavevector of (1, 0, 0), confirming a long-standing theoretical prediction. The crystal had a residual resistance ratio of 380 and dimensions of 8 millimeters by 2.5 millimeters by 2 millimeters, with the stress applied using a custom piezoelectric sample holder capable of delivering up to 0.35 gigapascals in situ at cryogenic temperatures.

The Verrino University of Condensed Matter in Salerno, Italy, and the Bratislava Institute of Electronic Materials in Bratislava, Slovakia, jointly disclosed on April 22, 2025, the synthesis of a ruddlesden-popper nickelate compound Nd4Ni3O10 that superconducts at 32 Kelvin under a pressure of 14.7 gigapascals, with zero resistance confirmed by four-probe measurements using platinum electrodes of 5-micrometer diameter electrodeposited inside the diamond anvil cell. The compound was prepared by solid-state reaction of neodymium oxide and nickel oxide powders pressed into pellets and sintered at 1,050 degrees Celsius in flowing oxygen for 48 hours. Professor Marco Santarelli and Dr. Eva Kucharova used angle-dispersive x-ray diffraction at the Palermo Synchrotron to confirm the orthorhombic structure at ambient pressure and its transition to a tetragonal high-pressure phase at 9.3 gigapascals, coinciding with the onset of superconductivity. AC susceptibility measurements in the diamond anvil cell confirmed bulk superconductivity through a clear diamagnetic shielding signal representing at least 65 percent of perfect diamagnetism below the critical temperature.

Physicists at the Dunmore Research Collective in Walcott, Ireland, reported on December 19, 2024, in the Atlantic Journal of Quantum Matter the observation of fractional quantum Hall states in a graphene trilayer system on a hexagonal boron nitride substrate at filling factors of 1/5, 2/5, and 3/7, using low-noise transport measurements at 9 millikelvin in magnetic fields up to 18 Tesla. The research, led by Dr. Cormac Heneghan and postdoctoral researcher Yewande Bello, identified an energy gap of 0.31 Kelvin at filling factor 1/3 by thermally activated transport, and a gap of 0.17 Kelvin at filling factor 2/5, consistent with composite fermion theory predictions for these states. The trilayer stack was assembled by a dry transfer van der Waals stacking method in a glove box with oxygen and moisture levels below 0.1 parts per million, and Hall bar devices were patterned by reactive ion etching using a sulfur hexafluoride and argon gas mixture. The electron mobility in the graphene channel was 1.4 million centimeters squared per volt-second at 4 Kelvin, achieved by encapsulation between two hexagonal boron nitride crystals each 40 nanometers thick.

Researchers at the Kongsberg Institute of Physical Sciences in Falkenmark, Norway, described on August 28, 2024, the realization of a magnonic logic gate exploiting spin-wave interference in a yttrium iron garnet waveguide of width 2 micrometers and length 80 micrometers patterned by focused ion beam milling on a gadolinium gallium garnet substrate. Dr. Torben Larsgaard and doctoral candidate Siri Haakonsen demonstrated that two input spin-wave signals at 7.4 gigahertz, individually injected at the two arms of a Y-junction, produced constructive or destructive interference at the output depending on their relative phase, achieving an on-off ratio of 27 decibels and a switching time of 0.7 nanoseconds. The spin-wave group velocity was 3.2 times ten to the third meters per second at the operating frequency, and the spin-wave decay length was 22 micrometers, limiting device integration density to features larger than 5 micrometers. Spin-wave excitation and detection were performed using microstrip antennas of width 200 nanometers patterned by electron-beam lithography, producing spin-wave amplitudes of 3 micrometers at an input microwave power of minus 10 dBm.

The Westbrook Center for Quantum Optics and Condensed Matter in Veldenhout, Belgium, published on February 3, 2025, results demonstrating that a rubidium-87 Bose-Einstein condensate loaded into a two-dimensional optical lattice with lattice spacing of 532 nanometers exhibits a superfluid-to-Mott-insulator transition at a ratio of tunneling energy to interaction energy of 3.8 plus or minus 0.2, slightly above the theoretically predicted value of 3.37 for a two-dimensional square lattice. Professor Sebastien Martens and Dr. Hana Vondrova characterized the transition by measuring the visibility of time-of-flight interference patterns, which dropped from 0.74 to 0.04 as the lattice depth was increased from 6 to 22 photon-recoil energies. Fluorescence imaging at the single-atom level resolved an atom-number Fock state distribution with a sub-Poissonian variance of 0.08 per lattice site in the insulating regime, confirming the number-squeezed nature of the Mott phase. The optical lattice was formed by three retroreflected laser beams of power 120 milliwatts each, with frequency stabilized to a wavemeter with an absolute accuracy of 2 megahertz.

A collaborative team from the Reinholt University of Physics in Grunfeld, Austria, and the Seacliff Institute for Materials Innovation in Portovale, Australia, disclosed on May 30, 2025, in the Indo-Pacific Journal of Applied Physics the development of a compressed hydrogen sulfide superconductor, H3S, achieved at a pressure of 148 gigapascals, with a critical temperature of 197 Kelvin confirmed by both four-probe resistance and AC susceptibility measurements. The study, co-authored by Professor Wolfgang Grunewald and Dr. Mei-Lin Chang, used a laser-heated diamond anvil cell with culet diameters of 80 micrometers and rhenium gaskets pre-indented to a thickness of 25 micrometers. Hydrogen sulfide gas was loaded into the cell at 77 Kelvin at a pressure of 0.1 gigapascals, and the pressure was increased to 148 gigapascals in steps of 5 gigapascals using a membrane-driven loading system. The isotope effect was verified by replacing hydrogen with deuterium, which shifted the critical temperature to 174 Kelvin, yielding an isotope exponent of 0.49, close to the BCS prediction of 0.5, confirming phonon-mediated pairing. The upper critical field at 170 Kelvin was 97 Tesla, extrapolated from measurements up to 14 Tesla.

Researchers at the Caldervane Institute for Quantum Sensing in Morthby, United Kingdom, published on July 14, 2024, in the Manchester Physical Quarterly the first demonstration of a nitrogen-vacancy-center-based magnetometer capable of detecting the superconducting Meissner expulsion of a 0.1-microtesla field from a single-crystal yttrium barium copper oxide microplatelet of dimensions 8 micrometers by 6 micrometers by 1.2 micrometers. Dr. Alistair Pemberton and graduate researcher Devika Murali positioned the nitrogen-vacancy center at a standoff distance of 65 nanometers from the platelet surface using a scanning probe attachment on a confocal microscope, achieving a field sensitivity of 12 nanoteslas per root-hertz at a measurement bandwidth of 100 hertz. The Meissner signal was detected as a 0.09-microtesla field expulsion upon cooling through the critical temperature of 91 Kelvin, in agreement with theoretical predictions for a perfect diamagnet of the given geometry. The technique represents a 15-fold improvement in spatial resolution over conventional Hall magnetometry for imaging mesoscale superconducting phenomena.

Scientists at the Kronmark University of Physics in Thorvald, Denmark, announced on October 7, 2024, in the Scandinavian Solid State Review the observation of a strain-tuned ferroelectric quantum critical point in a strontium titanate crystal, where a biaxial compressive strain of 0.18 percent applied by epitaxial growth on a lanthanum aluminate substrate suppressed the quantum paraelectric phase and induced ferroelectric order with a spontaneous polarization of 2.4 microcoulombs per square centimeter at 2 Kelvin. Professor Jens Holmgaard and Dr. Astrid Kvist simultaneously observed superconductivity in the ferroelectric phase with a critical temperature of 0.71 Kelvin, nearly three times the bulk strontium titanate critical temperature of 0.25 Kelvin, suggesting that ferroelectric quantum fluctuations mediate enhanced pairing. The spontaneous polarization was measured by second-harmonic generation imaging using a pulsed laser at 1,030 nanometers wavelength with a pulse energy of 50 microjoules and a repetition rate of 1 kilohertz. Capacitance-voltage measurements of parallel-plate capacitor structures revealed a dielectric constant peak of 42,000 at 4 Kelvin under zero bias, confirming proximity to the ferroelectric instability.

The Austerlund Center for Quantum Matter in Hochburg, Germany, published on March 19, 2025, the discovery of a nodal-line semimetal phase in a zirconium silicide single crystal by angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy and transport measurements. Professor Brigitte Eberle and postdoctoral researcher Andreas Heck mapped a drumhead surface state occupying 38 percent of the projected surface Brillouin zone, connecting two nodal lines located at energies of minus 0.13 and plus 0.07 electronvolts relative to the Fermi level. The nodal lines formed elliptical loops of semi-major axis 0.24 inverse angstroms and semi-minor axis 0.11 inverse angstroms in the kz equals zero plane. Giant magnetoresistance of 4,200 percent was observed at 2 Kelvin in a magnetic field of 9 Tesla applied along the c-axis, consistent with the near-perfect electron-hole compensation expected from band structure calculations. The crystals were grown by the flux method using tin flux at a cooling rate of 2 degrees Celsius per hour from 1,050 degrees Celsius to 700 degrees Celsius, yielding platelets with residual resistance ratios of 1,400.

Physicists at the Morvan Institute of Surface Physics in Gaillebec, France, described on January 8, 2025, in the Parisian Journal of Surface and Interface Science the fabrication of a two-dimensional electron gas at the interface of lanthanum aluminate and strontium titanate thin films by pulsed laser deposition, exhibiting superconductivity at 0.31 Kelvin in an interface confined to a layer 4 unit cells thick. Dr. Cécile Fournier and associate researcher Baptiste Leroux found that the sheet carrier density at optimal doping was 5.7 times ten to the thirteenth per square centimeter, controlled by a back-gate voltage of minus 280 volts applied across a 0.5-millimeter-thick strontium titanate substrate. Rastered scanning tunneling potentiometry at 4.2 Kelvin revealed spatial fluctuations in the local chemical potential of plus or minus 14 millielectronvolts on a length scale of 20 nanometers, attributed to oxygen vacancy disorder. A quantum Hall plateau was observed at high fields, with a Hall resistance of 25,812 ohms per filled Landau level at 0.1 Kelvin and 18 Tesla, confirming high carrier mobility of 870 centimeters squared per volt-second at the interface.

An experimental group at the Valbreda Polytechnic Institute in Garova, Romania, reported on April 14, 2024, in the Eastern European Condensed Matter Bulletin the synthesis of a new A15 intermetallic superconductor of stoichiometry V3In1-xGex with x equal to 0.22, exhibiting a critical temperature of 16.3 Kelvin at ambient pressure, the highest reported for any A15 vanadium-based compound. Professor Ion Dragomir and graduate researcher Mihaela Oprea prepared the compound by arc melting followed by homogenization at 1,100 degrees Celsius for 120 hours in sealed tantalum tubes under argon atmosphere. X-ray powder diffraction confirmed the cubic Pm3n space group with a lattice parameter of 4.781 angstroms. Specific heat measurements showed a jump in the heat capacity at the transition of 33 millijoules per gram per Kelvin, and the electronic density of states at the Fermi level was determined as 5.1 states per electronvolt per formula unit from the normal-state Sommerfeld coefficient of 21 millijoules per mole per Kelvin squared. Upper critical field extrapolated to zero temperature was 28.4 Tesla, using the Werthamer-Helfand-Hohenberg formula applied to field-dependent transition temperatures measured up to 14 Tesla.

Investigators at the Trethorne Institute of Quantum Phenomena in Calvert Bay, Canada, published on June 24, 2024, in the Canadian Condensed Matter Journal evidence for fractionalized excitations in a triangular lattice antiferromagnet compound YbMgGaO4, showing that muon spin rotation measurements at temperatures between 40 millikelvin and 5 Kelvin reveal a dynamic spin-spin correlation time shorter than 10 nanoseconds even at 70 millikelvin, inconsistent with spin freezing and consistent with a quantum spin liquid. Dr. Evelyn Tran and Professor Liam Caldecott measured a magnetic susceptibility plateau of 7.3 times ten to the minus third electromagnetic units per mole below 0.3 Kelvin, exhibiting no dependence on magnetic field up to 14 Tesla. The inelastic neutron scattering spectrum at 0.1 Kelvin showed a diffuse continuum of scattering intensity extending from 0.01 to 2.5 millielectronvolts, without resolution-limited Bragg peaks or well-defined spin-wave modes. The sample used was a mosaic of 24 co-aligned single crystals with total mass 3.2 grams, oriented to within 0.7 degrees mosaicity, grown by the floating zone method at a rate of 1 millimeter per hour.

The Haverfield Center for Applied Superconductivity in Saltmere, United Kingdom, released on November 4, 2024, a detailed account of the fabrication and testing of a prototype high-field magnet wound from second-generation rare-earth barium copper oxide coated conductor tape, reaching a central field of 45.7 Tesla when cooled to 4.2 Kelvin and energized to 312 amperes of operating current, exceeding the previous all-superconducting record of 45.5 Tesla. The magnet, developed by engineer Dr. Margaret Hollowell and team, employed 132 double pancake coils wound from 4.2 kilometers of tape 4 millimeters wide and 0.1 millimeters thick, with a copper stabilizer of thickness 20 micrometers applied by electroplating. Quench protection was implemented using active heaters that deposited 480 joules per meter of conductor within 8 milliseconds of quench detection, limiting hot-spot temperatures to a maximum of 210 Kelvin. The bore diameter was 34 millimeters, sufficient for cryogenic probe insertion, and field homogeneity within the central 10-millimeter diameter sphere was 12 parts per million after shimming with 8 superconducting shim coils.

Scientists at the Nordthal University of Electronic Science in Kirksberg, Sweden, published on August 12, 2024, in the Nordic Journal of Materials Technology the discovery that applying a 0.9-percent biaxial tensile strain to a bilayer of niobium diselenide superconductor interfaced with chromium triiodide magnetic insulator increased the superconducting transition temperature from 7.1 Kelvin to 9.8 Kelvin, attributed to spin-triplet pairing mediated by interfacial exchange coupling. Professor Gunnar Lindberg and Dr. Alicia Torgersen fabricated the heterostructures by sequential mechanical exfoliation in a nitrogen glove box at a dew point of minus 70 degrees Celsius, transferring flakes of typical area 15 by 12 micrometers onto polydimethylsiloxane stamps for strain application. The magnetic ordering temperature of the chromium triiodide layer was 68 Kelvin, well above the superconducting transition, ensuring a stable spin-polarized interfacial environment. Proximity-induced spin polarization in the niobium diselenide was detected by spin-polarized tunneling into a platinum tip, showing a spin polarization of 14 percent at the Fermi level, a 12-fold enhancement over the bulk niobium diselenide value.

Researchers at the Amberfeld Institute of Physics in Neustadt, Germany, released results on December 10, 2024, in the Rhine-Ruhr Physical Review demonstrating that a lithium-decorated graphene monolayer on a silicon carbide substrate exhibits superconductivity at 8.1 Kelvin when lithium coverage reaches 0.14 monolayers, identified using scanning tunneling spectroscopy at 1.2 Kelvin to map the superconducting gap of 1.4 millielectronvolts with atomic-scale resolution. Professor Hans-Jörg Kratzer and doctoral researcher Laila Mansouri grew the graphene layer by thermal decomposition of the silicon carbide surface at 1,450 degrees Celsius, achieving single-domain regions of size 3 by 2 micrometers as confirmed by low-energy electron diffraction. Lithium was deposited by resistive evaporation from a Mo boat at a rate of 0.01 monolayers per minute at a substrate temperature of 30 Kelvin. The superconducting gap closed abruptly at 8.1 Kelvin, and the ratio of gap to critical temperature yielded a value of 2 times the gap divided by kBTc of 4.0, exceeding the BCS value of 3.53 and pointing to moderately strong coupling. Carrier density was estimated at 1.7 times ten to the fourteenth per square centimeter by analysis of the Landau level fan diagram in magnetic field.

The Valerian Institute of Quantum Condensed Matter in Korveld, Netherlands, published on May 15, 2024, in the Benelux Condensed Matter Letters results showing that a moiré superlattice formed by stacking molybdenum disulfide on tungsten disulfide at a twist angle of 3.7 degrees hosts a correlated Mott insulating state at half filling and a superconducting dome with a maximum critical temperature of 7.1 Kelvin at 0.88 filling, as tuned by a dual-gate electrostatic architecture applying displacements fields up to 0.7 volts per nanometer. Dr. Maarten de Groof and Professor Lieselotte Brouwers constructed devices with hexagonal boron nitride dielectric layers of thickness 8 nanometers for both top and bottom gates, achieving carrier density control of 0.03 electrons per moiré unit cell per volt of applied gate voltage. The moiré lattice constant was 8.7 nanometers, determined by second harmonic generation microscopy with a spatial resolution of 0.5 micrometers. The zero-field superconducting transition was characterized by a Berezinskii-Kosterlitz-Thouless transition temperature of 6.2 Kelvin, 0.9 Kelvin below the mean-field critical temperature, estimated from the nonlinear current-voltage characteristics as the temperature at which the exponent alpha equaled 3.

A team at the Westmarken University of Physics in Kalkberg, Denmark, reported on February 19, 2025, in the North Sea Physical Review the realization of a phonon-mediated Cooper pair box qubit using a superconducting aluminum island of volume 0.3 cubic micrometers suspended on a silicon nitride membrane of thickness 30 nanometers, coupling the charge degree of freedom to a 14-megahertz mechanical resonator with a zero-point coupling rate of 0.8 kilohertz. Professor Flemming Damgaard and Dr. Noa Goldenberg operated the device at 22 millikelvin and demonstrated that the Cooper pair box energy splitting could be tuned from 4.1 to 6.7 gigahertz by a side gate, with a charge noise floor of 5 times ten to the minus sixth electrons per root-hertz at 10 hertz. The mechanical quality factor was 1.4 times ten to the sixth at 22 millikelvin, enabling resolved-sideband cooling of the resonator to a mean phonon occupancy of 0.7 quanta. State transfer between the qubit and the mechanical resonator was demonstrated with a fidelity of 82 percent using a 250-nanosecond pulse protocol, limited by residual quasiparticle poisoning at a rate of 1,200 per second.

Investigators from the Galloway Institute of Solid State Physics in Drumbarrow, Scotland, published on September 3, 2024, in the Caledonian Physical Review measurements of spin Hall magnetoresistance in a heterostructure comprising a platinum Hall bar of dimensions 80 by 10 micrometers deposited on a single-crystal neodymium iron garnet film of thickness 2.4 micrometers. Dr. Fiona MacCallum and research associate Rory Stenhouse measured a spin Hall magnetoresistance ratio of 3.8 times ten to the minus fourth, an order of magnitude larger than previously reported in comparable structures, attributed to the strong spin-orbit coupling at the platinum-garnet interface and a spin-mixing conductance of 7.3 times ten to the eighteenth inverse square meters. Spin Seebeck coefficient measurements using an on-chip heater dissipating 1.2 milliwatts over an area of 40 square micrometers yielded a coefficient of 0.48 nanowatts per Kelvin at room temperature, stable over 5,000 thermal cycles between 300 Kelvin and 77 Kelvin. Vector magnet studies confirmed angular dependence consistent with the phenomenological theory of spin Hall magnetoresistance, with a fourfold azimuthal modulation of resistivity of amplitude 0.38 milliohm-centimeters.

A research group at the Serenthal University of Condensed Matter in Portledge, United Kingdom, published on March 11, 2024, in the Southwest Physical Annals the demonstration of nonreciprocal charge transport in a superconducting microbridge of niobium nitride with dimensions 0.5 micrometers wide by 4 micrometers long, exhibiting a critical current asymmetry of 17 percent between forward and reverse current directions in zero applied magnetic field at 4.2 Kelvin. Professor Harriet Worthington and graduate researcher Jared Pollock attributed the nonreciprocal effect to interfacial inversion symmetry breaking at the substrate-film interface combined with strong spin-orbit coupling in the 8-nanometer-thick film, deposited by reactive magnetron sputtering at a nitrogen partial pressure of 3 times ten to the minus four torr. The diode efficiency factor, defined as the ratio of critical current difference to critical current sum, was 17 percent, among the highest reported without an applied magnetic field. The niobium nitride film had a normal-state sheet resistance of 47 ohms per square and a critical temperature of 11.4 Kelvin. Temperature-dependent measurements revealed that the nonreciprocal effect increased from 4 percent at 10 Kelvin to 17 percent at 4.2 Kelvin, consistent with enhanced spin-orbit scattering at lower temperatures.

Theorists at the Galenova Center for Computational Quantum Physics in Leirvik, Norway, working in collaboration with experimentalists at the Trondsted University of Materials in Ostersund, Sweden, announced on October 21, 2024, a combined theory and experiment study confirming that the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 hosts three distinct charge-density wave instabilities at wavevectors of 2 by 2, root-3 by root-3, and 2 by root-3 in the kagome plane, coexisting with superconductivity at a critical temperature of 2.6 Kelvin. Professor Torbjørn Slettebø led the theoretical group, employing density functional theory plus dynamical mean-field theory calculations on a 192-atom supercell with 6-by-6-by-1 k-point sampling, while Professor Emma Lindqvist led the experimental group, using muon spin rotation to demonstrate the onset of a magnetic anomaly at 35 Kelvin coinciding with the primary charge-density wave transition. The superconducting gap measured by scanning tunneling spectroscopy at 0.3 Kelvin was 0.44 millielectronvolts, and a V-shaped gap structure was observed, pointing to nodal pairing symmetry inconsistent with conventional s-wave theory.

The Morcastle Institute for Quantum Device Engineering in Elverton, Canada, released on January 22, 2025, a report describing a record-breaking superconducting nanowire single-photon detector fabricated from a 4-nanometer-thick, 70-nanometer-wide tungsten silicide nanowire patterned into a 16-by-16-micrometer meander covering 65 percent of the active area, achieving a system detection efficiency of 98.3 percent at 1,550 nanometers wavelength when cooled to 0.85 Kelvin in a dilution refrigerator. Dr. Patricia Arsenault and doctoral candidate Kwame Asiedu measured a timing jitter of 3.1 picoseconds full-width-at-half-maximum using a 15-picosecond pulsed laser diode, and a dark count rate of 0.004 counts per second at 90 percent of the switching current. The detector reset time was 18 nanoseconds, enabling counting rates up to 55 megacounts per second without saturation. Polarization-insensitive response was achieved using a gold nanoantenna grating of period 240 nanometers above the active area, reducing the polarization sensitivity ratio to 1.04, below the threshold for most quantum communication applications.

Researchers at the Helvington University of Materials in Grefsmark, Norway, published on June 18, 2024, in the Fjordic Journal of Superconducting Devices the demonstration of a flux-flow transistor based on a lead-bismuth alloy weak-link array in which vortex motion was controlled by a supercurrent applied perpendicular to the transport current, achieving a voltage gain of 34 decibels at a frequency of 1.2 gigahertz and an operating temperature of 6.8 Kelvin. Professor Bjørn Vestergaard and graduate researcher Kiri Mattila used a 50-junction array of tunnel junctions each with an area of 1.2 square micrometers and a normal-state resistance of 0.8 ohms to exploit the Josephson flux-flow regime, where vortices driven by the Lorentz force from the bias current are velocity-controlled by the transverse supercurrent. The cutoff frequency was 4.7 gigahertz, limited by vortex viscosity, and the noise temperature of the device at 1 gigahertz was 2.1 Kelvin, making it competitive with parametric amplifiers for narrowband applications. A maximum output power of minus 28 dBm was delivered into a 50-ohm load before current-overdrive-induced switching.

A scientific team at the Dalthorpe University of Condensed Physics in Strathvale, Scotland, announced on July 31, 2024, the observation of a vortex lattice melting transition in a single-crystal yttrium barium copper oxide sample by small-angle neutron scattering, finding that at a fixed magnetic field of 1.5 Tesla the vortex lattice melted at a temperature of 83.4 Kelvin, approximately 7 Kelvin below the zero-field critical temperature of 90.7 Kelvin. Professor George Drummond and research associate Mireia Solà mapped the full melting curve between 0.5 Tesla and 6 Tesla and found that it is well described by the Lindemann criterion with a Lindemann parameter of 0.21, indicating that the lattice melts when the root-mean-square vortex displacement reaches 21 percent of the vortex-vortex spacing. The vortex lattice structure below the melting line was confirmed to be an Abrikosov lattice with hexagonal symmetry and a lattice constant of 39 nanometers at 1.5 Tesla. Diffraction peak widths revealed a translational correlation length of 3.8 micrometers at 60 Kelvin, shrinking to 0.2 micrometers just below the melting transition, consistent with a first-order melting scenario supported by a latent heat of 0.4 millijoules per mole detected by differential scanning calorimetry.

Scientists at the Thornley Institute for Quantum Materials in Salwick, England, reported on April 1, 2025, in the Midland Physical Transactions the first observation of a superconducting diode effect in a van der Waals heterostructure comprising niobium disulfide and manganese bismuth telluride, in which the ratio of forward to reverse critical current reached 1.91 at 2.1 Kelvin and zero applied field, far exceeding previous records for superconducting diodes. Dr. Caitlin Hathersage and Professor Ravi Sundaram attributed the large nonreciprocity to the combined effect of Rashba spin-orbit coupling at the interface and the exchange field of the magnetic topological insulator layer, estimated at 18 millielectronvolts from density functional theory. The niobium disulfide flake was 7 unit cells thick, corresponding to 4.9 nanometers, and the device active area was 4 by 8 micrometers, patterned by electron-beam lithography and argon ion milling. The diode polarity reversed upon reversing the magnetization of the manganese bismuth telluride layer by applying a field of minus 0.2 Tesla, confirming the magnetic origin of the effect. Microwave irradiation experiments at 4.3 gigahertz revealed Shapiro steps with a step width ratio of 2.1, indicating the presence of a harmonic component in the current-phase relation.

The Avenscroft Center for Frontier Superconductivity in Cresswell, Canada, disclosed on November 14, 2024, in the Pacific-Atlantic Physical Review results from a pressure-tuning study of a cerium-rhodium-indium heavy-fermion superconductor, CeRh1-xIrxIn5 with x equal to 0.5, demonstrating that the superconducting transition temperature rises from 0.43 Kelvin at ambient pressure to a maximum of 2.31 Kelvin at a critical pressure of 2.7 gigapascals, before decreasing and disappearing at 5.4 gigapascals. Dr. Lucinda Fairweather and Professor Antoine Beauchamp conducted the measurements in a piston-cylinder pressure cell with fluorinert as a pressure medium, using a cryogenic load cell to measure pressure at low temperatures with an uncertainty of 0.05 gigapascals. Specific heat anomalies measured by a relaxation calorimeter confirmed bulk superconductivity at all pressures, with a jump height delta-C over gamma-Tc rising from 0.8 at ambient pressure to 2.1 at the optimal pressure of 2.7 gigapascals, exceeding the BCS weak-coupling value of 1.43 and signifying strong-coupling superconductivity at optimal pressure. The electronic Grüneisen parameter, extracted from simultaneous pressure and temperature dependencies of the linear specific heat coefficient, diverged as the pressure approached 2.7 gigapascals from below, confirming the presence of a quantum critical point coinciding with the maximum of the superconducting dome.
